program_outcomes: Matematik, fizik ve mühendislik bilimlerine özgü konularda yeterli bilgi birikimi; bu alanlardaki kuramsal ve uygulamalı bilgileri, karmaşık mühendislik problemlerinde kullanabilme becerisi.

Mühendislik tasarımında gerçekçi kısıtlar ve koşullar altında bilgisayar mühendisliği problemlerini saptama, tanımlama, formüle etme ve çözme becerisi; bu amaçla uygun analiz ve modelleme yöntemlerini seçme ve uygulama becerisi.

Yazılımsal veya donanımsal karmaşık bir sistemi, süreci veya cihazı gerçekçi kısıtlar ve koşullar altında, belirli gereksinimleri karşılayacak şekilde tasarlama becerisi; bu amaçla modern tasarım yöntemlerini uygulama becerisi.

Mühendislik uygulamalarında karşılaşılan karmaşık problemlerin analizi ve çözümü için gerekli olan modern teknik ve araçları geliştirme, seçme ve kullanma becerisi; bilişim teknolojilerini etkin bir şekilde kullanma becerisi.
Analitik düşünce ile bir sistemi, sistem bileşenini ya da süreci analiz etme, modelleme, deney tasarlama ve yapma, çözüm algoritmaları üretebilme, uygulamaya alma ve geliştirme becerileri.
Disiplin içi ve çok disiplinli takımlarda etkin biçimde çalışabilme becerisi; bireysel çalışma becerisi.

Türkçe sözlü ve yazılı etkin iletişim kurma becerisi; en az iki yabancı dil bilgisi; etkin rapor yazma ve yazılı raporları anlama, yazılım ve donanım tasarımını raporlayabilme, etkin sunum yapabilme, açık ve anlaşılır talimat verme ve teknik resim kullanarak iletişim kurabilme becerisi.
Bilgiye erişebilme ve bu amaçla kaynak araştırması yapabilme, veri tabanları ve diğer bilgi kaynaklarını kullanabilme becerisi.
Yaşam boyu öğrenmenin gerekliliği bilinci; kendini sürekli yenileme becerisi.
Etik ilkelerine uygun davranma, mesleki ve etik sorumluluk bilinci; mühendislik uygulamalarında kullanılan standartlar hakkında bilgi.
Proje yönetimi, risk yönetimi ve değişiklik yönetimi gibi, iş hayatındaki uygulamalar hakkında bilgi; girişimcilik, yenilikçilik hakkında farkındalık; sürdürülebilir kalkınma hakkında bilgi.
Mühendislik uygulamalarının evrensel ve toplumsal boyutlarda sağlık, çevre ve güvenlik üzerindeki etkileri ve çağın mühendislik alanına yansıyan sorunları hakkında bilgi.
Bilişim hukuku ve etik konular hakkında bilgi, bilgisayar mühendisliği çözümlerinin hukuksal sonuçları konusunda farkındalık.

To study a real function using the basic tools of mathematical analysis.

courses.name: Physics I
courses.description: Mathematical introduction, vector analysis, coordinate systems, applications of differential and integral calculus, and differential equations at a fundamental level for mechanics. Kinematics, dynamics, work and energy, linear momentum and collisions, rotational kinematics and dynamics, and oscillations including simple harmonic motion are covered.

courses.name: Physics I
courses.description: 1. Mathematical Introduction • Vector analysis (Scalar/dot and vector/cross products) • Cartesian and cylindrical coordinate systems • Applications of differential and integral calculus • Differential equations (Fundamental level for mechanics) 2. Kinematics • Motion in one dimension (Position, velocity, and acceleration vectors) • Motion in two and three dimensions (Projectile motion) • Uniform circular motion 3. Dynamics • Concept of force and free-body diagrams • Newton's Laws of Motion • Friction force and dynamics of circular motion (Centripetal force) 4. Kinetics (Work and Energy) • Work-Kinetic Energy Theorem • Conservative and non-conservative forces • Potential energy • Conservation of mechanical energy 5. Linear Momentum and Collisions • Center of mass (Transition from point particles to rigid bodies) • Linear momentum and Impulse • Conservation of linear momentum • Elastic and inelastic collisions  6. Rotational Kinematics and Dynamics • Rotational kinematics of rigid bodies • Moment of inertia and rotational kinetic energy • Torque and Newton's 2nd Law for rotational motion • Angular Momentum and its conservation • Rolling motion (Combination of translation and rotation) 7. Oscillations and Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) • Hooke's Law and restoring force • Kinematic equations of SHM (Time dependence of position, velocity, and acceleration) • Energy transformations and conservation in SHM • Applications: Simple pendulum and physical pendulum • Introduction to damped and driven oscillations, Resonance

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Engineering
courses.purpose: This course provides a comprehensive and interdisciplinary introduction to the broad spectrum of computer science and engineering. It aims to introduce students to the cornerstones of the field, from data storage to algorithms, and from software engineering to the theory of computation, so that they can understand the relationships between sub-disciplines and identify areas of specialization.
courses.description: Following the layered structure of a computer system, the course covers information and data representation, hardware and execution, software and operating systems, problem solving and algorithms, programming and abstraction, databases and software engineering, and theoretical limits and future topics such as artificial intelligence and ethics.
learning_outcomes: Gain a functional understanding of the computer engineering department and discipline.
Develop a historical perspective regarding computer engineering.
Comprehend how the hardware, software, and data layers of a computer operate as an integrated whole.
Evaluate fundamental computer systems, their inherent problems, and corresponding solution methods.
Explain core engineering concepts such as software development processes, database management, and computer networks using appropriate technical terminology.
Understand the theoretical limits of computability and the fundamental principles of contemporary technologies like Artificial Intelligence.

courses.name: Mathematics II
courses.purpose: This course deals in depth with the subject of linear algebra. Linear algebra is the basis of many techniques used in many fields such as computer science, automata and economics. Throughout the course, the basic concepts of linear algebra will be explored with an emphasis on real Euclidean spaces and vector spaces of polynomials.
courses.description: 1. Geometry of the plane and the space: Collinearity / orthogonality of the vectors of R ^ 2 or R ^ 3. 2. Geometry of the plane and the space: Application to the study to the study of the lines of the plane / of the lines and the planes of space 3. Linear systems: Gaus pivot method for solving linear systems. Geometric interpretation for systems with 2 or 3 unknowns. Discussion of the solutions of a system with parameters 4. Matrices: Definition and properties of operations on matrices. Matrix writing of a linear system. Reversible matirces. Linear application associated with a matrix. 5. Complex numbers: Cartesian and polar representation of a complex. Application to geometry and trigonometry 6. Complex numbers: Equation of degree 2 with complex coefficients. Nth roots of a complex. 7. Polynomials: Operations on polynomials. Euclidean division Roots of a polynomial 8. Partial examination / Arasinav 9. Polynomials: Taylor formulas. Factoring on C and on R 10. Vector spaces: Definition, examples and properties. Vector subspace of a vector space. 11. Vector spaces: Free families, generating families and bases of a vector space. 12. Vector Spaces: Dimensional theory. 13. Linear applications: Definition and properties. Matrix representation of a linear application. 14. Linear applications: Kernel and image of a linear application. Rank theorem. Change of bases.
learning_outcomes: solve a system of linear equations with the Gauss method and geometrically interpret the set of solutions
use Euclidean geometry in dimension 2 or 3 to solve a geometry problem
use complex numbers and their geometric representation to factorize a polynomial
Factorize a polynomial irreducibly or simplify a rational fraction
prove that a set is a vector space and determine its dimension
determine if any subspaces of a given vector space are supplementary
prove that an application is linear and write its matrix in given bases
find the kernel and the image of a given linear function
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Vector product and determinant in R^3. Lines and planes of space
Hafta 3: 2- Linear systems. Gaussian pivot method
Hafta 6: 4- Complex numbers Cartesian representation, polar representation
Hafta 9: 5- Polynomials Definition, operations, Euclidean division
Hafta 11: 6- Vector spaces. Definition, examples. Linear subspaces
Hafta 12: Linearly independent or spanning set of vectors. Basis.
Hafta 13: Dimension of a vector space
Hafta 14: 7- Linear applications Definition, examples. Matrix representation

courses.name: Physics II
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the universal laws of electromagnetic theory, from electrostatics and magnetostatics to electromagnetic waves. It also aims to build a solid mathematical foundation and develop problem-solving competence for applying electromagnetic concepts to engineering problems.
courses.description: Electrostatics: charge distributions, Coulomb's law, electric field, electric potential, Gauss's law, capacitance, and dielectric materials. Magnetostatics: magnetic field, Lorentz force, Biot-Savart law, and Ampere's law. Electrodynamics and induction: magnetic flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, motional emf, self-inductance, mutual inductance, and magnetic field energy. DC circuits: current, resistance, Ohm's law, emf, voltage, Kirchhoff's laws, Thevenin and Norton theorems. Maxwell's equations: displacement current, integral and differential forms, and their physical meaning. Electromagnetic waves: wave equation, plane wave properties, speed of light relation, Poynting vector, and electromagnetic spectrum.
learning_outcomes: Calculate the electric field and electrical potential for point and continuous charge distributions using Coulomb's and Gauss's laws.
Determine the magnetic fields generated by moving charges and currents using Biot-Savart and Ampere's laws; analyze the magnetic forces acting on charges.
Perform current, voltage, and equivalent resistance calculations in direct current (DC) circuits using Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's rules, and Thevenin/Norton theorems.
Model electromagnetic induction phenomena using Faraday's and Lenz's laws, and determine the induced electromotive force in dynamic systems.
Interpret Maxwell's equations in both integral and differential forms to summarize the dynamic interaction between electric and magnetic fields and the symmetry in nature.
Derive the electromagnetic wave equation from Maxwell's equations; evaluate the propagation of waves in a vacuum and energy transport using the Poynting vector.
resources: Lecture Notes and Exercises Moodle / Teams Learning Management Systems - LMS

courses.name: Physics II
courses.description: 1. Electrostatics: concept of charge (point, linear, surface, and volume charge distributions), Coulomb's Law, electric field and electric field lines, electric potential and potential energy, Gauss's Law and applications to symmetric charge distributions, capacitance, capacitors, and dielectric materials. 2. Magnetostatics: concept of magnetic field and magnetic force (Lorentz Force), magnetic effect of current (magnetic field of moving charges), Biot-Savart Law, Ampere's Law and applications. 3. Electrodynamics: induction: concept of magnetic flux, Faraday's Law of Induction, Lenz's Law (direction of induced current and conservation of energy), motional emf, self-inductance and mutual inductance, magnetic field energy. 4. Electric Circuits: Direct Current (DC) Circuits: current, current density, and resistance (Ohm's Law), electromotive force (emf) and voltage, Kirchhoff's Laws (Junction and Loop rules), Thevenin and Norton theorems. 5. Maxwell's Equations: displacement current and Ampere-Maxwell Law (creation of magnetic field by a time-varying electric field), integral and differential forms of Maxwell's equations: Gauss's Law for electricity, Gauss's Law for magnetism (absence of magnetic monopoles), Faraday's Law, Ampere-Maxwell Law. 6. Electromagnetic Waves: derivation of the electromagnetic wave equation from Maxwell's equations, properties of plane electromagnetic waves (orthogonality of E and B fields to each other and to the direction of propagation), relationship between the speed of light (c), electric permittivity (ε0), and magnetic permeability (μ0) of free space, Poynting Vector: energy transport and momentum in electromagnetic waves, electromagnetic spectrum.

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide foundational knowledge of how computer systems are designed, structured, and operate across hardware and software layers. It introduces students to the core components of computing systems, their interactions, and the fundamental principles underlying modern computing architectures.
courses.description: The course covers the physical foundations of computing (transistors and chip manufacturing), basic hardware components (CPU, GPU, memory, storage), abstraction layers in computer systems, digital logic fundamentals, instruction-level execution, memory hierarchy, input/output systems, operating systems basics, and an introduction to networking and system-level performance considerations.
learning_outcomes: Explain how modern computer systems are structured from hardware to software layers
Identify and describe the roles of core hardware components (CPU, memory, storage, I/O devices)
Understand binary representation and basic digital logic operations
Describe how instructions are executed within a processor
Explain memory hierarchy and performance trade-offs
Understand the basic role of operating systems in resource management
Interpret simple performance metrics and system-level bottlenecks
Develop a conceptual framework for further studies in computer engineering
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Computer manufacturing processes, semiconductor basics, transistor technologies
Hafta 2: Introduction to computer hardware: CPU, GPU, RAM, storage devices
Hafta 3: Abstraction layers in computer systems
Hafta 4: Binary numbers, data representation, and number systems (decimal, binary, hexadecimal)
Hafta 5: Digital logic fundamentals: logic gates, Boolean algebra, combinational circuits
Hafta 6: Sequential logic and basic circuit design: flip-flops, registers, finite state machines
Hafta 8: Introduction to computer architecture: instruction sets, machine language, assembly basics
Hafta 9: CPU organization: datapath, control unit, instruction execution cycle (fetch-decode-execute)
Hafta 10: Memory systems: cache, main memory, virtual memory, memory hierarchy
Hafta 11: Storage systems and I/O: disks, SSDs, peripherals, buses, and communication between components
Hafta 12: Introduction to operating systems: processes, threads, scheduling, and memory management
Hafta 13: System performance: benchmarking, latency vs throughput, bottlenecks, optimization basics
Hafta 14: Emerging trends in computer systems: parallel computing, GPUs, cloud computing, and edge systems
resources: Computer Systems, 5th Edition J. Stanley Warford

courses.name: Advanced Mathematics I
courses.purpose: This course is the continuation of the Math I course. It aims to equip students with classical techniques for calculating primitives, understanding comparison relations between functions, and determining the nature of generalized integrals and numerical series.
courses.description: 1. Primitives: Definition, properties and first examples. 2. Primitives: Calculation rules [integration by parts and change of variable] 3. Comparison relations: function negligible in front of another, function equivalent to another 4. Comparison relations: calculation rules, comparative growth of logarithms, powers and exponential in 0 and infinity. 5. Comparison relations: Application to the search for limits. 6. Generalized integrals: definition, properties and first examples [Riemann integrals and Bertrand integrals]. 7. Generalized integrals: comparison theorems for positive functions. 8. Generalized integrals: case of functions of any sign. 9. Partial Examination / Ara sinav 10. Generalized integrals: Integrals depending on a parameter 11. Numerical series: definition, properties and first examples [Riemann series and Bertrand series]. 12. Numerical series: comparison theorems for series with positive terms. 13. Numerical series: Case of series of any sign. Convergence criterion of alternating series. 14. Digital Series: Series depending on a parameter
learning_outcomes: Make an integration by parts and / or a change of variable to calculate the integral of a function
Compare two functions at a given point
Determine a "simple" equivalent of a function to calculate its limit at a point
Apply comparison theorems to determine if a positive function admits a generalized integral
Know how to use a Taylor expansion to determine the nature of an integral [absolutely convergent, semi-convergent or divergent]
Apply comparison theorems to determine if a series with positive terms is convergent
Know how to use a lTaylor expansion to determine the nature of a series [absolutely convergent, semi-convergent or divergent]

Hafta 10: Generalized integrals: Definition, Interpretation and Properties
Hafta 11: Generalized integrals: Case of positive functions

courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the mathematical foundations needed to solve problems involving linear systems, matrix diagonalization, and related applications in physics, statistics, and engineering. Determine the eigenspaces of a matrix.

Solve linear systems (equations differential or recurrent sequences).

courses.description: The course covers the concept of probability, especially random variables in relation to uncertain events; different probability distributions; the use of probability theory in problems encountered in the business world, especially in the analysis of uncertainty; and probability concepts in industrial applications.

courses.name: Analogical Electronics
courses.purpose: The primary objective of this course is to provide students with a comprehensive engineering vision that extends from basic circuit theory to the physics of semiconductor devices and the design of modern analog electronic systems. The course aims to develop the competence to model, analyze, and design analog circuits using a mathematical approach.
courses.description: This course covers the analysis of DC circuits, transient response of first- and second-order circuits, AC and sinusoidal steady-state behavior, frequency response and filters, fundamentals of semiconductor physics, diodes and their applications, transistors, and operational amplifiers. It includes rectifier, amplifier, filter, and regulator circuit design for processing continuous analog signals.
learning_outcomes: Mathematically analyze the behavior of RLC circuits in transient (time domain) and sinusoidal (frequency domain) regimes.
Derive the transfer functions of passive filter topologies and interpret the frequency responses of systems by plotting and reading Bode diagrams.
Explain the physical behavior of the P-N junction by comparing the energy band structures of conductors, insulators, and semiconductors.
Analyze and design rectifier, ripple filter, and voltage regulation circuits using ideal and practical diode models.
Determine the operating regions of Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJT); model basic amplifier circuits by performing biasing operations on the DC load line.

Design analog signal processing circuits (adder, subtractor, differentiator, and integrator) using the characteristics of an ideal operational amplifier (Op-Amp) and the principle of negative feedback.

courses.name: Analogical Electronics
courses.description: 1. Review: Electric Circuits: Direct Current (DC) Circuits • Current, current density, and resistance (Ohm's Law) • Electromotive force (emf) and voltage • Kirchhoff's Laws (Junction and Loop rules) • Thevenin and Norton theorems 2. Transient Response • First and second-order circuits (RC, RL, and RLC) • Charge/discharge curves and time constant concept • Step and pulse responses of circuits 3. Alternating Current and Sinusoidal Steady-State • Complex numbers and phasor concept • Impedance and admittance • AC Power (Active, reactive, apparent power, and power factor) • Series and parallel resonance in RLC circuits 4. Frequency Response and Filters • Transfer function concept • Bode plots (Drawing and reading magnitude and phase curves) • Passive filter topologies: Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, and band-stop filters • Cutoff frequency and bandwidth calculations 5. Fundamentals of Semiconductor Physics • Energy band structures of conductors, insulators, and semiconductors • Intrinsic semiconductors and electron-hole concept • P-type and N-type doping • P-N junction and depletion region formation  6. Diodes and Applications • Ideal and real diode characteristics (Current-Voltage, I-V curve) • Rectifier circuits: Half-wave and full-wave (bridge) rectifiers • Ripple voltage reduction with filter capacitor • Zener diodes and voltage regulation • Clipper, clamper circuits, and LEDs 7. Transistors • Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJT): NPN and PNP structures • BJT operating regions (Cutoff, Saturation, Active region) • BJT biasing circuits and DC load line • Transistor logic as a switch and amplifier • Introduction to Field Effect Transistors (FET/MOSFET) 8. Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps) • Ideal Op-Amp characteristics and equivalent circuit • Negative feedback principle and virtual short circuit • Basic Op-Amp configurations: Inverting and non-inverting amplifiers • Adder, subtractor, and voltage follower (buffer) circuits • Integrator and differentiator circuits (Electronic equivalent of mathematical operations)

courses.name: Advanced Mathematics II
courses.purpose: Today, many branches of science—from operations research to statistics and economics—require the use of functions of several variables. Bilinear algebra is a fundamental tool in the analysis of these functions. Quadratic forms arise when one seeks an approximate representation of a multivariate function. In this context, determining whether a function has a minimum amounts to checking whether the quadratic form associated with the function is positive. Bilinear algebra also makes it possible to solve minimization problems by transforming them into problems of finding the shortest distance from a point to a set. Thus, when orthogonality is ensured, the minimum point is attained.
courses.description: Bilinear forms and inner product; pre-Hilbert spaces and Euclidean spaces; orthonormal bases and Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization; orthogonal complement and orthogonal projection; applications such as least squares and approximation of a periodic function; diagonalization of symmetric matrices; norms on vector spaces and equivalence of norms in finite dimensions; continuity of functions of several variables; partial derivatives and differential of multivariable functions; curves and surfaces including level curves, gradient vector, and tangent plane; minimum and maximum of multivariable functions.
learning_outcomes: Verify whether a given quadratic form defines an inner product and construct an orthonormal basis using the Gram-Schmidt process.
Determine the orthogonal complement of a vector subspace and use orthogonal projection to compute the distance between a vector and a subspace.
Diagonalize a symmetric matrix of dimension 3 or 4 by computing an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
Select an appropriate norm to study the continuity of functions of two or three variables and analyze the existence and continuity of their partial derivatives at a given point.
Compute the differential of a multivariable mapping.
Determine the gradient vector of a function, deduce a normal vector to level curves, and compute the tangent plane to an implicitly defined surface.
Determine local extrema of a function of two variables using the Hessian matrix.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Bilinear forms and inner product
Hafta 2: Pre-Hilbert spaces and Euclidean spaces
Hafta 3: Orthonormal bases for an inner product, Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization process
Hafta 4: Orthogonal complement of a vector subspace and projection
Hafta 6: Applications: least squares, approximation of a periodic function
Hafta 9: Norms on a vector space, equivalence of norms in finite dimensions
Hafta 10: Continuity of a function of several variables
Hafta 11: Partial derivatives and the differential of a multivariable function
Hafta 12: Curves and surfaces: level curves, gradient vector, and tangent plane
Hafta 13: Minimum and maximum of a multivariable function

courses.name: Differential Equations
courses.purpose: The course aims to introduce students to the theory and methods of differential equations, showing that some equations cannot be solved explicitly and that the notion of solution can be subtle. It also aims to develop students' ability to solve linear differential equations and systems, and to perform qualitative analysis of differential equations.
courses.description: First-order linear differential equations; solution of second-order homogeneous and non-homogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients; second-order linear differential equations with variable coefficients; examples of nonlinear first-order differential equations; linear differential systems with constant coefficients; equilibrium points of two-dimensional differential systems.
learning_outcomes: Ability to solve first- and second-order linear differential equations, whether homogeneous or non-homogeneous, with constant or variable coefficients.
Ability to use the method of variation of constants to solve differential equations.
Ability to determine maximal solutions for first-order linear differential equations.
Ability to sketch phase portraits or integral curves associated with differential equations.
Ability to distinguish between different types of equilibrium points.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: First-order linear differential equations: structure of the solution set and solution methods.
Hafta 2: Solution of first-order linear differential equations using the method of variation of constants.
Hafta 3: First-order linear differential equations: study of matching (patching) problems.
Hafta 4: Solution of second-order homogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients.
Hafta 5: Solution of second-order linear differential equations with constant coefficients using the method of variation of constants.
Hafta 6: Second-order linear differential equations with constant coefficients: study of matching problems.
Hafta 7: Solution of second-order linear differential equations with variable coefficients, including an adapted use of the variation of constants method.
Hafta 9: Study of examples of nonlinear first-order differential equations.
Hafta 10: Solution of homogeneous linear differential systems with constant coefficients, with applications.
Hafta 11: Solution of linear differential systems using the method of variation of constants.
Hafta 12: Analysis of equilibrium points of two-equation differential systems.
Hafta 13: Continued study of equilibrium points of two-equation differential systems.

courses.name: Object Oriented Programming
courses.purpose: The primary objective of this course is to provide students with a deep understanding of the Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigm and principles, which are at the heart of modern software development processes. Throughout the course, students aim to analyze problems from an object-oriented perspective, decompose complex software systems into manageable components, develop reusable and flexible code, and gain proficiency in modeling system architecture via UML diagrams.

Use an object-oriented programming language and associated class libraries to write and test programs.
Develop UML-based designs using the concepts of encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism to solve real-world problems.
Implement UML-based designs (based on class diagrams) for problems displaying architectural complexity and significant inter-class communication.
Use IDEs (Integrated Development Environments) to design, implement, debug, and execute object-oriented applications.

courses.name: Discrete Mathematics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the necessary arithmetic and information theory background to examine systems such as error correction codes and Markov chains. It also introduces the mathematical foundations needed to understand system modeling in data transfer, data storage, and related applications.
courses.description: Arithmetic topics include the extended Euclidean algorithm, greatest common divisor, Diophantine equations, congruence systems, and the convergence speed of the Euclidean algorithm. The course also covers error correction codes, including Hamming distance, linear codes, generator and control matrices, syndrome-based correction, cyclic codes, generating polynomials, and Markov chains with transition matrices, convergence, and boundary configurations.
learning_outcomes: Apply the Euclidean algorithm to find the Bézout relation between two integers.
Solve equations with integer coefficients and congruence systems.
Calculate the Hamming distance of an error correction code and determine the number of errors it can correct.
Determine whether a code is linear or cyclic, and calculate its generator matrix, control matrix, syndrome, and generator polynomial.
Determine whether an event can be modeled with a Markov chain, calculate the transition matrix, and find the limit configuration of a Markov process if any.
Calculate the weights of a stochastic matrix.

Hafta 11: Markov chains: Introduction and first examples
Hafta 12: Markov chains: Transition matrix and transition diagram of a Markov chain
Hafta 13: Markov chains: Convergence theorem of transition matrices
Hafta 14: Markov chains: Search and interpretation of boundary configurations

courses.name: Operating Systems
courses.purpose: This course focuses on the fundamental concepts and mechanisms of operating systems, including processes, memory management, I/O management, file systems, and inter-process communication and synchronization. It also aims to help students apply these concepts through laboratory work using the C programming language.
courses.description: Introduction, OS types, basic concepts, and course scope; OS components, hardware classes, and file systems; processes, threads, system calls, and kernel/user-level context switching; synchronization primitives and producer/consumer pattern; scheduling; security and protection; virtual memory; efficient cache management, consistency and coherence; high-performance locks, fair scheduling, deadlocks, and livelocks; dynamic memory management; linking, dynamic libraries, and deployment; virtual machines, containers, jails, and sandboxing. Laboratory topics include Linux fundamentals, introduction to Pintos, process/thread work, context switching, mutexes, semaphores, mutex lock ordering, shared mutex, and deadlock.
learning_outcomes: Ability to explain the operation of a standard operating system.
Ability to use and craft userspace or kernelspace code in low-level C.
Ability to explain and use multi-process programming.
Ability to analyze, explain, and implement inter-process synchronization requirements.
Ability to effectively manage hardware resources like memory, bandwidth, and processor.

Hafta 3: Process, Thread, System Calls, Kernel and User-level Context Switching
Hafta 10: Efficient Cache Management, Consistency and Coherence
Hafta 11: High-Performance Locks, Fair Scheduling, Deadlocks, Livelocks

After each theoretical lesson, what is learned in the lesson will be applied on one of the database management systems widely used in the industry. Possibility to make a backup of the database and to restore from this backup

courses.name: Advanced Databases
courses.purpose: This course is designed for students with good basis in programming, as well as good knowledge of relational data model, relational algebra and a broad knowledge of the relational DBMS. The objective of this course is to situate and understand the tools of access to relevant information and develop an analytical framework and keys to comprehend the company’s needs in a project of Data Warehousing. It provides a strong competency on distributed system data storage units, query all types of databases, transform different types of data on these databases and integrate them on a single data warehouse, as well as model data warehouse and teaches reporting and questioning suitable for business intelligence to be used in business life. At the same time, it aims to provide the student with competence on Big Data architecture, analytics and data flow.
courses.description: 1. Introduction, basic concepts, Data types 2. Basic concepts of business intelligence, introduction to OLAP systems 3. Data warehouse architecture and principles 4. Data warehouse modeling 5. ETL applications, basic concepts and tools 6. Data analysis, creating OLAP cubes, querying 7. Hierarchy, KPI and Calculation definition and MDX queries 8. Introduction to Data Engineering 9. Big Data: Basic Concepts - Introduction to RTAP systems 10. Big data ecosystem: Hadoop, HDFS, YARN and MapReduce algorithms 11. Data pipline and Data Ingestion 12. Lambda Architecture 13. Data Processing Methods 1) Streaming processing with Kafka, Flink, Spark 2) Batch processing with HDFS, Hive, Spark 14. Big Data Analytics on cloud systems
learning_outcomes: Can work on distributed system databases.
Can design and model data warehouse.
It can transform different types of data to come together on a single warehouse.
Can create OLAP cubes.
Ad-hoc reports.
Design and implement distributed systems suitable for Big Data Architecture.
Can manage real-time data flow, create a data line to ensure data flow.
Can do big data analytics on cloud systems.

courses.name: Medical Informatics
courses.purpose: To learn and understand the principles and characteristics of medical informatics. It also presents the effect of novel IT approaches on medicine and healthcare.
courses.description: This course is an introductory course on medical informatics. It introduces the concept of computer usage in medical domain with its history, current applications and future trends.
learning_outcomes: understand the need and benefits of using information and communication technologies in the field of medicine
will be able to interpret the technological solutions applied for the problems encountered in this field and propose new ones
process and appropriately display medical data
will be able to bring innovative approaches in the field of medical informatics
acknowledge and elaborate on the ethical and professional issues with required background knowledge
perform effectively in a project team
acquire and apply new knowledge from the given resources

Hafta 11: Hospital Information Systems and PACS

Object Transformation Functions, Projection Designs 6. Object Animation 7. Animation Models 8. Midterm Exam 9. Object-Oriented Graphic Design 10. Interactive OpenGL Programming 11. Introduction to Different OpenGL Derivatives: WebGL, OpenGLES, GLSL, JavaScript 12. Game Engine Architectures 13. 3D Scene Design, Ray Tracer 14. Projects
learning_outcomes: They can propose appropriate software architectures for developing visual applications based on different object and graphic models.
They have gained analytical skills through effective representations in the field of graphic design.
They can list approaches that can be applied in the design and storage of multi-dimensional objects.
They can develop different approaches to solving current and complex problems related to object design and graphic representation.

courses.name: Software Engineering and Object Oriented Design
courses.purpose: This course introduces tools that can be used in the object-oriented design process and encourages students to develop the ability to apply these tools in practice. It also helps students understand the role of software engineering in computer engineering, the necessity of software design, and the use of UML and other methods throughout the software development life cycle.
courses.description: Introduction to software engineering and design, agile project management, software requirements analysis, conceptual design of software, software design principles, technical design, UML class diagrams, design patterns, project conceptual design presentations, software quality, software testing techniques, software development models, estimation in software projects, and term project presentations.
learning_outcomes: By understanding the responsibilities of a software engineer, the student will be able to document and report a software problem, its requirements, and the corresponding software and hardware design in both textual and visual forms.
The student will be able to prepare functional analysis documents, requirements documents, and design documents for a software system.
The student will be able to select and apply the most appropriate software development process for developing a software system.
By knowing different software testing methods, the student will be able to choose and apply the most suitable testing technique for a given software system.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Software Engineering and Design, Introduction to Agile Project Management
Hafta 4: Software Design Principles, Technical Design
resources: Software Engineering, Ian Sommerville, Addison-Wesley, 10th Edition, 2015.
Introduction to Software Engineering Design, Processes, Principles, and Patterns with UML2, Christophe Fox, Addison-Wesley, 2006.

courses.name: Fundamentals of Embedded System Design
courses.purpose: The aim of the Embedded Systems and Software course is to teach students end-to-end embedded system design. Within this process, students will learn to define the requirements of the system they will design, select appropriate external hardware and microprocessors, analyze the system’s power consumption and cost, anticipate the environmental impacts of the system they develop, and ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations.
courses.description: Within this course, students are introduced to embedded systems, energy sources, batteries, consumption, costs, and environmental impacts. The course covers the design process, requirement specification, optimization, project selection, standards, regulations, laws, embedded software development, programming hardware components such as I2C, EEPROM, SPI, and UART, communication with peripherals, real-time systems, interrupts and response times, power consumption analysis, Internet of Things, future embedded system technologies, productization, testing, and verification.
learning_outcomes: Be able to recall general knowledge about embedded systems, evaluate their environmental impacts, and design ecologically responsible approaches.
Be able to identify appropriate hardware and standards for use in embedded systems.
Be able to define and exemplify application areas of embedded systems.
Be able to determine system requirements and specify detailed design considerations.
Be able to make predictions about the economic lifetime of systems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Energy sources, batteries, consumption, costs, and environmental impacts
Hafta 3: Design process, requirement specification and optimization - Project selection
Hafta 5: Introduction to embedded software development
Hafta 6: Programming hardware components - I2C, EEPROM, SPI, UART
Hafta 10: Different types of interrupts and response times
Hafta 11: Hardware and software analysis of power consumption
Hafta 13: Future of embedded systems, various embedded system technologies, translating learning into products
Hafta 14: Embedded system testing and validation
resources: Making Embedded Systems: Design Patterns for Great Software (Paperback)

courses.name: Graduation Project
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to enable students to analyze, design, implement, and evaluate a real engineering problem by integratively applying the theoretical knowledge and technical skills they have acquired throughout their computer engineering education. Within the scope of the course, students develop a software or hardware system individually or in teams under the supervision of a faculty advisor. The course also aims to provide students with experience in project management, technical reporting, presentation skills, and engineering ethics.
courses.description: Scientific research process, identification of the research problem, preparation of the first draft of the report. Discussion of the selected project topics, determination and presentation of the project objectives. Establishing the project work schedule, basic information on the use of project management tools. Preparation of the project plan. Conducting an academic literature review, identifying similar and existing studies, preparing a literature review report, and proper citation of sources. Determining the tasks to be carried out in the project and the technologies to be used, identifying the project components. Designing the project, defining workflows and usage requirements, basic information on the use of existing project design tools. Preparation of the second interim report. Preparation of the third interim report.
learning_outcomes: Clearly define the problem that constitutes the subject of the study.
Provide a solution to the problem using a computer engineering technique.
Interpret the results obtained with the help of comparisons and scenario analyses.
Apply the proposed approach to real-life examples.
Conduct a literature review.
Develop skills in preparing and delivering technical presentations and posters.
Improve the ability to present a technical topic within the allocated time.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Scientific research process, identification of the research problem, preparation of the first draft of the report.
Hafta 2: Discussion of the selected project topics, determination and presentation of the project objectives.
Hafta 3: Establishing the project work schedule, basic information on the use of project management tools.
Hafta 4: Preparation of the project plan.
Hafta 5: Conducting an academic literature review, identifying similar and existing studies, preparing a literature review report, and proper citation of sources.
Hafta 6: Determining the tasks to be carried out in the project and the technologies to be used, identifying the project components.
Hafta 7: Designing the project, defining workflows and usage requirements, basic information on the use of existing project design tools.
Hafta 9: Preparation of the second interim report.
Hafta 14: Preparation of the third interim report.
resources: Selected topic-related resources and lecture notes

courses.name: Operations Research
courses.purpose: Operations research aims to apply scientific approaches in order to design and manage systems with scarce resources. This course enables students to provide optimal solutions to problems encountered in organizations and to improve and optimize organizational performance. It also aims to help students formulate mathematical models for real-world problems, learn solution methods for mathematical programming models, and interpret model validity and results.
courses.description: Introduction to linear programming and graphical solution methods, example problems for linear programming, simplex method, two-phase simplex method, special cases for simplex method, sensitivity analysis, assignment problem and Hungarian algorithm, transportation problem and transportation simplex algorithm.
learning_outcomes: Formulate linear programming models.
Calculate solutions to linear programming problems with simplex method.
Detect special cases arising during the application of the simplex method.
Calculate the effects of the change in model parameters on the optimal results.
Calculate the optimal solutions of transportation problems.
Calculate the optimal solutions of assignment problems.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Operations Research: definition and history
Hafta 4: Linear Programming and Graphical Method
Hafta 9: Problems in Linear Programming and Simplex Method
resources: Hillier, F.S., Lieberman, G.J., “Introduction to Mathematical Programming”, McGraw-Hill, 1995.
Bazaraa, M.S., Jarvis, J.J., Sherali, H.D., “Linear Programming and Network Flows”, John Wiley & Sons, 1990.
Taha, H.A., “Operations Research: An Introduction”, Sixth edition, Prentice-Hall, 1997.

courses.name: Engineering Economy
courses.purpose: The course aims to develop a better understanding of the basic theoretical results in engineering economics and their applications. It also seeks to provide students with a foundation in the mathematical tools used to compare economic alternatives and to understand the assumptions and limitations of engineering economy.
courses.description: Introduction to engineering economy, present worth analysis, annual worth analysis, rate of return analysis for single and multiple alternatives, incremental analysis, evaluation tools for alternatives, replacement and retention decisions, selection from independent projects under budget limitation, effects of inflation, depreciation methods, and after-tax economic analysis.
learning_outcomes: Explicates the principles of basic engineering economy.
Analyzes daily life and business problems based on selection of alternatives by using economic fundamentals.
Evaluates the alternatives according to cost or return.
Analyzes replacement decisions.
Synthesizes after-tax economical data with the help of gained techniques.
resources: Tolga, Ethem ve Kahraman, Cengiz., Mühendislik Ekonomisi, İTÜ Yayınları, No: 1542, 1994
Leland T. Blank, Anthony Tarquin, Basics of Engineering Economy, McGraw Hill, 2007.

courses.name: Cloud Computing
courses.purpose: This course aims to study cloud computing as a distributed systems paradigm and to provide both theoretical and practical knowledge on virtualization, containerization, microservices architectures, Kubernetes, scalability engineering, observability, DevOps, cloud security, and cost optimization. It equips students with the ability to design, deploy, and manage scalable, reliable, secure, and cost-efficient cloud-native systems.
courses.description: Cloud as a distributed systems paradigm; virtualization and containerization; cloud networking architecture; cloud storage systems; scalability engineering; distributed systems; microservices architecture; Kubernetes architecture; autoscaling and scheduling; observability and reliability engineering; DevOps and Infrastructure as Code; cloud security architecture; cloud economics and cost engineering; serverless and edge computing.
learning_outcomes: Analyze cloud computing within the context of distributed systems.
Explain and implement virtualization and container technologies.
Design cloud networking and storage architectures.
Develop scalable and highly available systems.
Deploy and manage microservices architectures and Kubernetes-based systems.
Configure autoscaling and scheduling mechanisms.
Apply observability and reliability engineering principles.
Implement DevOps practices and Infrastructure as Code for CI/CD pipelines.
Design secure cloud architectures and perform risk assessments.
Conduct cloud cost analysis and optimization.
Evaluate serverless and edge computing paradigms.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Cloud as a Distributed Systems Paradigm
resources: Patni, Sakshi, Deepika Saxena, and Ashutosh Kumar Singh. Resource Management in Cloud Computing. 2025.
Ferreira, Haroldo. Cloud computing. Editora Senac São Paulo, 2025.

courses.name: Introduction to Generative Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to enable students to grasp the fundamental mathematical, algorithmic, and engineering principles underlying generative artificial intelligence systems; and to teach how large language models (LLMs), image and text generation models, and modern generative architectures work, how they are trained, how they are evaluated, and how they can be deployed in real-world applications.
courses.description: Content (Below) It can be found in the topics section.
learning_outcomes: Explain the mathematical foundations underlying generative AI systems.
Describe the architectures of large language models (LLMs) and retrieval/agent architectures.
Train and optimize modern generative AI systems using appropriate techniques.
Evaluate the performance and quality of generative AI systems using relevant metrics and methodologies
weekly_topics: Hafta 5: Attention Mechanism and Transformer Fundamentals
Hafta 6: Transformer Decoder and LLM Architecture
Hafta 13: Tool Calling and Agentic Systems
resources: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch), Sebastian Raschka, September 2024

courses.name: Wireless and Mobile Networks
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to provide theoretical and practical knowledge in the field of wireless and mobile network technologies. It aims to enable students to understand technologies such as Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular networks, sensor networks, and Mobile IP, while gaining analytical skills in critical concepts such as security and Quality of Service (QoS).
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to wireless and mobile network technologies. The fundamental principles of wireless communication, protocols, architectures, and standards are examined. The course focuses on topics including Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, cellular networks (from 1G to 5G), wireless sensor networks, Mobile IP, ad hoc networks, wireless network security, and quality management. Emphasis is placed on both theoretical knowledge and real-world applications.
learning_outcomes: Explain the fundamental principles and protocols of wireless communication.
Compare Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular network technologies.
Analyze mobility management and wireless routing protocols.
Evaluate security and Quality of Service (QoS) issues in wireless and mobile network environments.
Design a wireless network system, conduct research, and manage a project-based learning process through individual or teamwork within a real-world context.
weekly_topics: Hafta 3: Characteristics of the wireless channel
Hafta 7: Topics at the edge network
Hafta 13: Advanced topics, Open and Private 5G networks
resources: Jim Kurose, Wireless and Mobile Networks Course Notes, The Computer Networks Research Group, University of Massachusetts
Jochen Schiller, Mobile Communications, 2nd Edition, Addison-Wesley, 2003.
Yi-Bing Lin & Imrich Chlamtac, Wireless and Mobile Network Architectures, Wiley, 2001.
William Stallings, Wireless Communications and Networks, Upper Saddle River, N.J: Prentice Hall, 2002.

courses.name: Social Media
courses.purpose: The main objective of the course is to enable students who have received intensive and only engineering training for four years to become aware of the effects of the products and applications they develop on society and the environment. The course also aims to make students aware of social media reflections in sociology, law, psychology, anthropology, communication, and information technology, and to introduce them to the history, sociological power, media power, and data-related aspects of social media.
courses.description: This course covers the relationship between technology and society, the historical development of social media, the social factors shaping social media, the effects of social media platforms on individuals and social values, the role of engineers, virtual reality representations, participatory culture on YouTube, community relations, social media as a public sphere, media convergence, otherness and virtual lynching, social networking sites as virtual communities and marketing communication channels, and social media from a data science perspective.
learning_outcomes: Ability to examine and explain the impact of social media on society
Ability to explain and criticize the media dimension of social networks
Ability to create and publish a blog as a social media tool
Ability to explain and criticize the technological dimension of social media
Ability to process and interpret big data obtained through social media tools
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Social Media - General Course Introduction
Hafta 2: Definition of "technology" and its relation to society
Hafta 3: A historical look at the relationship between technology and society: Social factors that shape the concept of social media
Hafta 4: The roles played by social media platforms in transforming social values
Hafta 5: The Effects of Social Media Platforms on the Individual
Hafta 6: The roles of engineers and the roles of engineers in the relationship between technology and society
Hafta 7: Information Society Reality Experiences: Representation of Virtual Reality in Fiction
Hafta 8: Youtube as a participatory culture
Hafta 9: Community relations on social media
Hafta 10: Social media as a new public sphere
Hafta 11: Media Convergence with Social Media and Internet Technology
Hafta 12: Social Media as a Media of Otherness: Virtual Lynching and Otherization
Hafta 13: Social networking sites as a type of virtual community - functioning as a marketing communication channel
Hafta 14: Review of Social Media from a Data Science Perspective

courses.name: Knowledge Discovery and Introduction to Data Mining
courses.purpose: This course aims at giving a general perspective and the ability of creating applications about data mining, which is an important task of advanced computer science, to the students. The real-world problems related to association rule mining, clustering and classification will be analyzed and possible solutions to those problems will be examined. Hence, the students will be able to propose practical solutions to data analysis problems.
courses.description: Fundamentals of Data Mining. Data preprocessing: cleaning, normalization, binning, standardization, discretization, reduction. Association rule mining: Apriori algorithms, FP-Growth algorithm, other algorithms. Classification: fundamentals, decision tree, Bayesian classification, neural networks. Clustering: fundamentals, distance, partitioning algorithms, hierarchical algorithms, grid and density based algorithms. Advanced topics in data mining: sequential pattern mining, text mining.
learning_outcomes: Having knowledge about the fundamentals of knowledge discovery and data mining
Ability of understanding real-world data mining problems and proposing solutions to them
Ability of understanding foremost trends of data mining and creating theoretical background for adapting them
Understanding the roles of association rule mining, classification and clustering and ability of applying them to different problems
Ability of programming basic algorithms of data mining in statistical programming languages
Interpreting results of data mining algorithms

Hafta 3: Data Preprocessing 2 - standardization, discretization, reduction
Hafta 4: Association Rule Mining 1 - Apriori Algorithms
Hafta 5: Association Rule Mining 2 - FP-Growth Algorithm, other algorithms
Hafta 10: Clustering 1 - Fundamentals, Distance, Partitioning Algorithms
Hafta 12: Clustering 3 - Grille and Density based Algorithms
Hafta 13: Advanced Topics in Data Mining 1 - Sequential Pattern Mining, Project Presentations 1
Hafta 14: Advanced Topics in Data Mining 2 - Text mining, Project Presentations 2
